title: Review: Supergirl is not the disaster its low box office suggests
gist: Warner Bros.' Supergirl, the DCU follow-up to Superman, is a solid-but-not-great film that flopped on opening weekend, with the reviewer attributing the miss to broad superhero fatigue, a plot spoiled by trailers, and Gunn-Gillespie creative friction rather than culture-war backlash.

insights.claim: Supergirl posted a very disappointing opening weekend that fell short of Warner Bros.' expectations for its follow-up to Superman, despite generally positive critical reception.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: A second straight DCU launch underdelivering signals the franchise reboot is not yet converting critical goodwill into theatrical revenue, a risk for WBD's studio segment.
insights.quote: a very disappointing opening weekend box office—not the outcome Warner Bros. was hoping for with this follow-up to last year’s Superman

insights.claim: The reviewer identifies ongoing superhero fatigue as the chief cause of Supergirl's weak box office, arguing an over-saturated superhero market makes 'pretty good' films insufficient to pull audiences from streaming into theaters.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Genre saturation as the binding constraint means even competent comic-book output faces a demand ceiling; watch theatrical vs streaming release strategy for future DC/Marvel titles.
insights.quote: there are likely several contributing factors, ongoing superhero fatigue chief among them
insights.locator: So why isn’t the film measuring up financially?

insights.claim: Weak pre-existing demand hurt the film because fans weren't clamoring for a standalone Supergirl movie and the character barely appeared in the prior Superman.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Spin-offs launched without an established audience runway carry elevated flop risk; a signal to scrutinize character seeding before greenlighting standalones.
insights.quote: Fans weren’t exactly clamoring for a standalone Supergirl movie; the character barely appeared in Superman last year.
insights.locator: So why isn’t the film measuring up financially?

insights.claim: Marketing undercut the film because the trailers gave away essentially the entire, already-predictable plot.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Over-revealing trailers erode theatrical urgency; a recurring, fixable marketing failure mode worth tracking across studio campaigns.
insights.quote: the trailers pretty much gave away the entire plot, which is completely predictable anyway
insights.locator: So why isn’t the film measuring up financially?

insights.claim: The Hollywood Reporter cites competing versions of the film and 'creative differences' between director Craig Gillespie and DC Studios head James Gunn as contributing factors to the underperformance.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Director-vs-showrunner friction inside the rebooted DCU is a production-risk flag for the slate Gunn is overseeing.
insights.quote: the Hollywood Reporter cites competing versions and the proverbial “creative differences” between Gillespie and Gunn as contributing factors
insights.locator: So why isn’t the film measuring up financially?

insights.claim: The reviewer explicitly rejects 'anti-woke' sentiment, misogyny, or the lead actress's appearance as explanations for the low box office, contradicting the online-troll narrative.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Attributing flops to structural genre/market factors rather than culture-war causes reframes how studios should diagnose and de-risk future releases.
insights.quote: the low box office is not because of “anti-woke” sentiments, misogyny, or Milly Alcock’s teeth
insights.locator: So why isn’t the film measuring up financially?

insights.claim: Multiple big-budget 2026 films underperformed alongside Supergirl—Masters of the Universe, The Mandalorian and Grogu, and Disclosure Day all fell short of expectations, with only Disclosure Day recouping its production budget.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: A cluster of tentpole misses points to a broad theatrical demand problem in 2026, not one studio's mistake—bearish read-through for large-franchise theatrical bets.
insights.quote: Masters of the Universe, The Mandalorian and Grogu, and Disclosure Day all fell short of expectations, although the latter at least recouped its production budget
insights.locator: Supergirl isn’t the only big-budget film to underperform

insights.claim: Original, non-franchise films Backrooms and Obsession are the standout box-office breakout hits so far in 2026, signaling audiences are hungering for something different.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: The winners are original IP, not sequels/franchise extensions—an actionable shift in where theatrical upside is concentrating.
insights.quote: Backrooms and Obsession are the big breakout hits thus far. Audiences are clearly hungering for something different
insights.locator: Supergirl isn’t the only big-budget film to underperform

insights.claim: Supergirl is part of the DCU's 'Gods and Monsters' chapter under the James Gunn and Peter Safran 'soft reboot,' which repurposed a script (by Ana Nogueira) originally written for the abandoned DCEU.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Warner is building its new DC continuity partly on recycled legacy assets—informs how quickly and cheaply Gunn/Safran can populate the slate.
insights.quote: The project was reimagined when James Gunn and Peter Safran took over and launched the “soft reboot” DCU.
insights.locator: The studio tapped Ana Nogueira to write the script

insights.claim: Warner Bros. has already committed to continuing the character, with Milly Alcock's Supergirl set to return in next year's Man of Tomorrow.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: The DCU roadmap is locked in despite the weak opening, so a soft box office won't immediately reset the slate—watch Man of Tomorrow as the next demand test.
insights.quote: we’ll see Alcock’s Supergirl again in next year’s Man of Tomorrow
insights.locator: Supergirl is currently playing in theaters

insights.claim: The reviewer predicts retrospective critical assessments of Supergirl will be kinder than the current reception, implying long-tail catalog/streaming value beyond the theatrical window.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: A film underrated at release can still accrue value via streaming and catalog longevity—relevant to how WBD monetizes the title post-theatrical.
insights.quote: It might not be an instant DCU classic—although I think retrospective assessments will be kinder
insights.locator: Supergirl is currently playing in theaters

insights.claim: Supergirl improves on its predecessor by keeping a straightforward plot and avoiding the super-character cameo overload that weighed down last year's Superman.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: A leaner narrative approach is being validated critically even as it fails commercially—suggests story quality is not the binding constraint on box office.
insights.quote: The plot is refreshingly straightforward without overpacking itself with super-character cameos like its predecessor.

insights.claim: The film leans on a darker, antihero tone for Kara Zor-El and the reviewer flags Krem of the Yellow Hills as a dull one-note villain, marking villain-writing as a weak spot despite strong lead performances from Milly Alcock and Eve Ridley.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Casting delivers but antagonist development lags—a repeatable execution gap to weigh when assessing DCU sequel prospects.
insights.quote: Krem of the Yellow Hills is a rather dull one-note villain, despite Schoenaerts’ valiant efforts to make him something more
insights.locator: Alcock, best known as the young Rhaenyra Targaryen
